title: Multi-Agent Cyber Defense with Multi-Level Zero-Trust Actions
abstract: The rise of distributed and decentralized systems has rendered existing perimeter-based cybersecurity approaches insufficient. This paper explores the application of zero-trust security principles in such environments, where no user or device is inherently trusted and continuous authentication is enforced. We model this challenge using a Decentralized Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (Dec-POMDP) to capture the dynamics of multi-agent cyber defense under uncertainty. We introduce and evaluate a structured set of zero-trust actions spanning three critical defense layers: (1) identity-based controls via dynamic user-level access policies, (2) network isolation through micro-segmentation, and (3) deception and obfuscation via intelligent decoy deployment. These actions empower defenders to make localized, trust-informed decisions and improve responsiveness in environments with limited visibility. Analytical results demonstrate that augmenting the defenders’ action space with zero-trust strategies guarantees measurable gains in expected system value. Leveraging the CAGE4 cybersecurity testbed, empirical findings show that zero-trust actions reduce attacker success rates, improve containment time, and enhance zone-level recovery following compromise, achieving over 25% improvement in cumulative defensive reward under partial observability.
